title: Traveling with Kyle - Bolivia Part 2 - Copacabana, La Paz, Potasi Mountain

transcript.text: Yeah, and he also had a story of how he was a scientist in the North Pole, and he, like, was in a caravan of, like, trucks that were, like, you know, doing tests, and there were these two polar bears who were, like, stalking them, trying to hunt people that were in their huge caravan of cars.

So they had four helicopters, and two of them chased these polar bears away, and then they parked the helicopters, and the polar bears came back and completely destroyed the helicopters, the very two helicopters that chased them away. Somehow they recognized the helicopters. They remember the serial number or something, and so they had to dismantle the helicopters and take them out in their caravan, and he had another story that he was, like, in Guatemala, like, helping, like, you know, victims of the Civil War, and so I guess some people, some Guatemalans didn't like him for exposing them or something,
So they shot him in the leg to scare him away, and so he, like, walked a little limp, and he had to, like, leave at that time or something, but anyway, interesting guy.

So anyway, I go back to Copacabana, and I hang out there for another couple days in this deserted Copacabana, and there's this one hotel that, like, you could rent movies or whatever, and they said that I'd ride, it was time for me to ride my bike to La Paz, so I ride my bike, and I go over some big trail, some big hill to over the south side of the lake, where you pay some guy to take a little ferry across 100 meters,

Across to the other side, but I was riding, like, I ride my bike up the hill, and I had to, like, weave in and out of these, like, stones that all the campesinos put, but I was on my bike, so I was free to go anywhere I wanted to, whereas all the other tourists were kind of stuck in Copacabana until this week or two week long, and, like, strike was over.
But I did go by another group of, like, rocks, and I look up, and I see, like, 50 campesinos on top of a cliff right above me, all with rocks above their head.
One guy raises his hand like he's going to throw me, and I'm like, whoa, hold on, no, no, I'm just a dude.

And then he kind of puts the rock down, and I wave at all of them, and they all wave at me, and then I rode by, and then, yeah, I do that little ferry thing across, and I ride across, and then I'm riding across Santa Plana towards La Paz, and I see that the whole road is just packed with people.

It's just all these people on bikes, no cars, and I ride alongside some, like, 50, 60-year-old Indian guy who's like, yeah, I'm riding my bike because I have to because, you know, all of the trucks, all the buses, the bus drivers are on their strike, and so I have to get to work anyway.
And he said he claimed he was riding 60 miles each way or something to work.
I mean, he probably didn't understand what a mile or kilometer was, but it was still pretty impressive.

And then I was also, I'm running down the road, and I look over, and there's, like, nobody for hundreds of meters below in front of or behind me, and I look over, and I see this Indian kid, this Incan kid, on his back with a backpack on, so his head is still back.

And apparently he had, he was riding his bike, and he got an epileptic seizure and fell off his bike because he's, like, all his blood is coming out of his knots, because I guess he hit his face when he landed.
And he's all, like, shake, sheezering, going, and, but he was choking to death.
He was going, he was, like, swallowing his, his blood and his tongue and just couldn't breathe.
So I jump on my bike, and I grab him, and I pull him on his side, and I, and so he's allowed to start breathing then.
But he keeps shaking for another few seconds, and then he stopped shaking, and the weirdest thing, like, he was, like, I was, like, are you okay?
Are you okay?
And he just, he was not able to talk.
He just, like, looked at me and just, like, looked past me for, like, a bunch of minutes.
He was just, like, this.
And then a bunch of, and then I noticed a few hundred meters bike behind me that there were people on bikes, and the bike people finally came up, and they got off their bikes.
And I told them that this kid, like, had an epileptic seizure or whatever, and so they, like, grabbed him and kind of, um, whatever.
So then I took off, but, um, and then I rode my bike into, uh, and then, and then right when I got into La Paz, that's where the cars were actually still driving.
So there was cars driving, and then I rode my bike, and then I, I, I'd heard about La Paz.
La Paz is probably the most visually stunning city on the whole planet.
Whoa, 423, are you crazy?
Um, because it's, like, there's the Bolivian antiplano, which is at, like, 13,800 feet.
Like, almost 14,000 feet, I think it is.
Whole, just the flat, like, you know, it's too high for, um, for any trees to grow.
That's how high it is.
And that's where Tiwanaku is, by the way, which is, which I guess at the time was kind of maybe in the south end of the lake or something.
It's maybe 20 miles south of the lake.

Like, and it's these huge stones, it's just kind of, like, Sac-Sac-Waman giganta stones that are all, and there's a huge block of stone that you can actually see a photo of that's 100, that's, like, I don't know how high it is, 20 tons or something.
It's, like, six feet by five feet by five feet placed on top of these other huge giganta stones.
And inside of them, they have, like, iron hooks that hook them together.
Nobody knows how it was built.
Nobody knows how they could have had a civilization that was strong enough to drag those stones from who knows where.
That at that altitude, whatever, it was the aliens, I think, but whatever, I don't know.
So anyways, yeah, but I heard about, but anyways, you have this anti-plano, and then you look towards it, and then you see these big mountains behind it.
And on the other side of the mountain is the jungle, which is way down below it.

But, so, like, you have no idea that there's a city until you get right up to the edge of, like, almost a cliff where it just drops down, and the bottom of this really steep valley is this huge megalopolis with skyscrapers and everything.
Huge city.
It's tricky to look down at, you know, like, whoa!
Ta-da!
A city in the middle of nowhere.

It's kind of like that movie The Phantom Menace, and they swim under that lake, and there's that, you know, there's that, all of a sudden, there's that megalopolis, you know, on the bottom of the lake.

So then I ride my bike down, and it was, like, down to a riverbed, which originally was a river that went through this valley, but now the river is completely underground, and you have an absolutely zilcho trace of the river.
It's just where the river used to be.
It's just the super, you know, central road.
And then, you know, farther down, the river pops out completely black with shit, you know.
It's like the sewage system or whatever.
It smells bad, but anyways, I went down there, and I got a hotel room, and then this other guy who I knew kind of showed me about this other hotel room, which was closer up.
And up on the side of the hill, and it was interesting.
You walk up and down, like, there's the road on the bottom, and then on either side, and especially on the western side, you walk up, and the road's, like, this steep, you know.
And this is like, you're talking 13,500 feet, so even if you're acclimatized, you're out of breath walking up and down these roads.
And, you know, they had even homeless people sleeping on the streets, literally, at that altitude through the winter.
I don't know how they survived.
And old people, too.
There's, like, this 80-year-old lady living on the street, and she looked miserable.
I gave her a, like, an Incan llama jacket, which I think it looked like she needed, and it looked like she appreciated it.
And then I tried to take a photo of her, and she's like, no, no photo!
And I'm like, hold on there, sister.
I just gave you a jacket.
So she's like, okay.
So I took a photo.

But, like, I got a hotel room that was right on the witch's market, this little alley where the witch's market is, and where they sell all sorts of crazy shit, like, uh, like, um, bobcat skins, even though bobcats are elite, are endangered and illegal to hunt.
They use them for some, um, mystical, superstitious thing.
Um, and they only were selling it for, like, 20 bucks, too.
Pretty cheap stuff.
But they had all sorts of trinkets and such.
And they were doing, and they, like, make little fires to burn.
Maybe that's how they got rid of their trash.
I don't know.
But I should have talked to them.
I should have even read them.
I just wasn't that outgoing at the time.
Maybe I was still reeling from being so high so long.
But it would have been interesting to see all the stuff they did believe.
But I did pay some guy to do a tea leaf prophecy for me, where this, like, guy who could barely even speak Spanish.
A lot of those Indians, they're Aymara and Inca, and they speak their native language still.
And they, you know, they're really, they're more than Spanish.
So, like, their Spanish is really broken and only talking the present tense and such.
But he gives me this, he, like, looks at the organization of the tea leaves, and he tells me that I was going to have money problems or something and something else.
But I don't really remember.
Anyway, I stayed at a hotel room, and, um,
It was, like, three bucks and I was like, good deal.
And I had a nice room.
Uh, anyway, um,
I, uh, I was there, I hung out with some people.
I met some guys.
The guy who I met in Lake Titicaca on the sailboat, and we, and these other two Argentinian guys,
So I guess he was friends with, because he had, like, a restaurant there or something.
We went and got some guides to take us up to climb this mountain called Potasi,
Which is the 6,000-meter mountain,
A 20,000-foot mountain,
Which is a pretty hike.
They'd drive us all the way up, and then we'd hike up.
And the very, the very end of it was, like, this steep.
We were roped up, and, like, it was, like,
If you go up, you'd almost get, like, um, um, you know,
You'd almost get, what's that word?
Vertigo, because it's so steep.
And the very top of it, the flat part was, like, smaller than this room.
It was just a couple feet by a couple feet.
And there was lots of people jam-packed up there.
You know, tons of tourists were climbing this mountain.
But the view was probably the most amazing view of any mountain in the whole world,
Because you could see to the north, east, west, the, uh, Lake Titicaca,
The entire of Lake Titicaca with Isla del Sol.
You could see the highest mountain in all of, uh, Bolivia, um, 6,300 meters high.
Mountain something or other that was way off in the distance, um, to the southwest,
Which would look like a perfect cone-shaped volcano, a bunch of other mountains.
Iliamani, the 6,400-meter mountain.
Oh, yeah, the other one's 6,900 meters.
It was really up.
And then Iliamani, 6,400 meters to the south.
And then you could see to the east.
Oh, and then La Paz, of course, which is amazing to see from up there,
Down in its little valley.
And then it goes up to these mountains.
And then on the western side, eastern side,
Ooh, I got to see.
Oh, yeah.
